    Assistant A

    Awesome. Let’s do the whole stack properly.

    I’ll cover:

    1. Three.js material/shader compilation pipeline
    2. glTF binary layout: buffers, bufferViews, accessors
    3. PBR equations in glTF metallic-roughness
    4. Tangent-space normal mapping derivation
    5. Skinning math and inverse bind matrices
    6. PMREM / split-sum approximation for IBL
    7. Dissect an actual GLB file byte-by-byte
    8. Full glTF PBR shader math
    9. How to inject custom GLSL into the Three.js viewer

    I’ll keep this dense and technical.

    1. Three.js material / shader compilation pipeline

    Three.js looks “high-level”, but under the hood it is doing a surprisingly serious shader pipeline.

    1.1 What actually happens when you render a mesh

    Suppose you have:

    JavaScript
    const mesh = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material);
scene.add(mesh);
renderer.render(scene, camera);

    The renderer does roughly:

    1. Traverse visible scene graph
    2. Collect renderable objects
    3. Sort draw lists
    4. For each object:
      • determine effective material state
      • determine geometry attributes
      • determine lighting/environment/fog/clipping/skinning/morph state
      • build or reuse a WebGLProgram
      • bind uniforms and textures
      • bind vertex attributes / index buffer
      • issue WebGL draw call

    1.2 The “program key” idea

    Three.js does shader specialization.

    A MeshStandardMaterial is not “one shader”. It is a template that becomes many shader variants depending on features.

    A variant key includes things like:

    • material type
    • skinning?
    • morph targets?
    • morph normals?
    • vertex colors?
    • fog?
    • clipping planes?
    • shadow maps?
    • environment map?
    • normal map?
    • tangent space?
    • number/type of lights?
    • alpha test?
    • tone mapping?
    • color space handling?
    • instancing?
    • transmission / clearcoat / sheen / iridescence extensions?

    So conceptually:

    ProgramKey=f(material,scene lights,renderer state,geometry attrs,extensions)\text{ProgramKey} = f(\text{material}, \text{scene lights}, \text{renderer state}, \text{geometry attrs}, \text{extensions})ProgramKey=f(material,scene lights,renderer state,geometry attrs,extensions)

    Then:

    • same key → reuse shader program
    • new key → compile new program

    This is why shader permutation explosion is real.

    1.3 Shader assembly in Three.js

    Three.js does not store one monolithic GLSL file for each material. It builds shader source from chunks.

    For example, a shader might contain lines like:

    glsl
    #include <common>
#include <uv_pars_fragment>
#include <normal_pars_fragment>
#include <lights_pars_begin>
#include <map_fragment>
#include <normal_fragment_maps>
#include <lights_fragment_begin>

    These are replaced with actual GLSL snippets at compile time.

    This means the final fragment shader is a concatenation of:

    • preprocessor defines
    • precision declarations
    • uniforms/varyings
    • reusable chunks
    • material-specific body

    This modular chunk system is what makes onBeforeCompile possible.

    1.4 Core internal stages

    Very roughly, the path looks like this:

    A. Material setup

    Material properties determine defines/uniforms:

    • material.map
    • material.normalMap
    • material.roughness
    • material.metalness
    • material.skinning
    • etc.

    B. WebGLPrograms parameter extraction

    Three computes something like:

    • shader ID
    • defines
    • precision
    • clipping state
    • tone mapping
    • light counts
    • env map mode
    • instancing/skinning/morph flags

    C. Program cache lookup

    A hash key is generated.

    D. GLSL source generation

    Three expands shader chunks, prepends #defines, injects version/precision, etc.

    E. WebGL compile/link

    • create shader objects
    • compile vertex shader
    • compile fragment shader
    • link into program
    • query attribute/uniform locations

    F. Runtime binding

    For each draw:

    • bind program
    • upload changed uniforms
    • bind textures
    • bind VAO / attributes
    • issue drawElements or drawArrays

    1.5 Why onBeforeCompile works

    Because shaders are generated as strings before compile.

    When you do:

    JavaScript
    material.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
  shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(...);
};

    you intercept the generated source after chunk expansion and define setup, but before compilation.

    This is extremely powerful but has constraints:

    • string replacement is brittle
    • chunk names can change between Three.js versions
    • custom uniforms must be manually managed
    • material clones may duplicate callbacks in tricky ways

    1.6 Uniform binding model

    At draw time, Three updates uniforms based on:

    • camera matrices
    • object matrices
    • material params
    • lights
    • env map / fog / clipping
    • skinning matrices / bone textures

    If a value didn’t change, Three tries to avoid redundant uploads.

    Uniform categories include:

    Object-local

    • modelMatrix
    • normalMatrix

    Camera/global

    • viewMatrix
    • projectionMatrix
    • camera position

    Material

    • base color
    • roughness
    • metalness
    • map transforms
    • alpha cutoff
    • emissive

    Lights

    • arrays/structs for directional/point/spot light params
    • shadow matrices/maps

    1.7 Attribute binding

    A geometry exposes attributes like:

    • position
    • normal
    • uv
    • tangent
    • color
    • skinIndex
    • skinWeight

    These map to shader inputs.

    In WebGL terms, Three binds:

    • array buffer
    • element buffer
    • enables vertex attrib arrays
    • sets pointer/stride/offset/divisor

    If instancing is active, some attributes get divisor =1=1=1.

    1.8 Why first render hitches

    Because on first use, all of this may happen:

    • shader compile
    • program link
    • texture upload
    • buffer upload
    • PMREM generation
    • environment convolution
    • shadow map creation

    That is a pile of CPU + GPU setup.

    2. glTF binary layout: buffers, bufferViews, accessors

    This is the most important low-level glTF concept.

    glTF is essentially:

    • JSON scene description
    • binary blobs
    • typed interpretations of slices of those blobs

    2.1 The three-layer memory model

    buffer

    Raw binary payload.

    Example:

    JSON
    "buffers": [
  { "byteLength": 8400 }
]

    Think: untyped byte array.

    bufferView

    A slice of a buffer.

    Example:

    JSON
    "bufferViews": [
  {
    "buffer": 0,
    "byteOffset": 0,
    "byteLength": 2880,
    "target": 34962
  }
]

    This says:

    • use buffer 0
    • start at byte 0
    • length 2880 bytes
    • intended for vertex attributes (ARRAY_BUFFER)

    So:

    bufferView=buffer[offset:offset+length)\text{bufferView} = \text{buffer}[offset : offset+length)bufferView=buffer[offset:offset+length)

    accessor

    Typed interpretation of a bufferView.

    Example:

    JSON
    "accessors": [
  {
    "bufferView": 0,
    "componentType": 5126,
    "count": 240,
    "type": "VEC3"
  }
]

    This says:

    • use bufferView 0
    • interpret bytes as FLOAT32 (5126)
    • there are 240 elements
    • each element is vec3

    Thus total bytes expected:

    240⋅3⋅4=2880240 \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 2880240⋅3⋅4=2880

    Perfectly matches the bufferView.byteLength.

    2.2 Why this indirection exists

    Because one binary blob can hold many kinds of data:

    • positions
    • normals
    • UVs
    • indices
    • animation keyframes
    • inverse bind matrices

    The indirection lets glTF reuse shared binary data efficiently.

    2.3 Common componentTypes

    • 5120 = BYTE (int8)
    • 5121 = UNSIGNED_BYTE (uint8)
    • 5122 = SHORT (int16)
    • 5123 = UNSIGNED_SHORT (uint16)
    • 5125 = UNSIGNED_INT (uint32)
    • 5126 = FLOAT (float32)

    Element “shape” is given by type:

    • SCALAR
    • VEC2
    • VEC3
    • VEC4
    • MAT2
    • MAT3
    • MAT4

    2.4 Stride and interleaving

    A bufferView may be tightly packed or interleaved.

    tightly packed

    All positions contiguous:

    text
    p0 p1 p2 p3 ...

    interleaved

    Each vertex stores multiple attributes:

    text
    [p0 n0 uv0] [p1 n1 uv1] [p2 n2 uv2] ...

    Then byteStride is used.

    Example:

    JSON
    {
  "buffer": 0,
  "byteOffset": 0,
  "byteLength": 3200,
  "byteStride": 32,
  "target": 34962
}

    A position accessor over this view might read 12 bytes every 32 bytes.

    Mathematically:

    addr(i)=base+byteOffset+i⋅stride\text{addr}(i) = base + byteOffset + i \cdot strideaddr(i)=base+byteOffset+i⋅stride

    2.5 Accessor min/max

    Position accessors often include:

    JSON
    "min": [-1, -1, -1],
"max": [ 1,  1,  1]

    Useful for:

    • bounds
    • culling
    • framing camera
    • analytics

    2.6 Normalized integer attributes

    An accessor may specify normalized: true.

    That means integer data is mapped to floating range in shader interpretation.

    Examples:

    • uint8 normal map-ish data for vertex colors or normals
    • skin weights in normalized bytes

    For unsigned integer:

    xfloat=xint2b−1x_{float} = \frac{x_{int}}{2^b - 1}xfloat​=2b−1xint​​

    For signed normalized:

    xfloat=max⁡(xint2b−1−1,−1)x_{float} = \max\left(\frac{x_{int}}{2^{b-1}-1}, -1\right)xfloat​=max(2b−1−1xint​​,−1)

    2.7 Index buffers

    Primitive indices use accessor too.

    If component type is UNSIGNED_SHORT, each index is 2 bytes.

    A triangle list with count = 3000 indices means:

    3000/3=1000 triangles3000/3 = 1000 \text{ triangles}3000/3=1000 triangles

    2.8 Sparse accessors

    A sparse accessor stores:

    • mostly default/base data
    • sparse overrides at selected indices

    Useful for morph targets or sparse modifications.

    Conceptually:

    A[i]={override[j]if i=indices[j]base[i]otherwiseA[i] = \begin{cases} override[j] & \text{if } i = indices[j] \\ base[i] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}A[i]={override[j]base[i]​if i=indices[j]otherwise​

    3. PBR equations in glTF metallic-roughness

    Now the serious part.

    glTF’s core material is based on:

    • baseColor
    • metallic
    • roughness
    • normal
    • occlusion
    • emissive

    Its reflectance model is a metallic-roughness microfacet BRDF.

    3.1 Core decomposition

    Outgoing radiance:

    Lo(v)=∫Ω+fr(l,v) Li(l) (n⋅l) dωlL_o(\mathbf{v}) = \int_{\Omega^+} f_r(\mathbf{l},\mathbf{v})\, L_i(\mathbf{l})\, (\mathbf{n}\cdot \mathbf{l})\, d\omega_lLo​(v)=∫Ω+​fr​(l,v)Li​(l)(n⋅l)dωl​

    with BRDF split into:

    • diffuse
    • specular
    fr=fd+fsf_r = f_d + f_sfr​=fd​+fs​

    3.2 Metallic workflow intuition

    A material is either:

    • dielectric-ish: nonmetal
    • conductor-ish: metal
    • or blend between

    Dielectric

    • diffuse reflectance present
    • specular F0≈0.04F_0 \approx 0.04F0​≈0.04

    Metal

    • little/no diffuse
    • specular colored by base color

    So glTF uses:

    F0=(1−m)⋅0.04+m⋅baseColorF_0 = (1-m)\cdot 0.04 + m \cdot \text{baseColor}F0​=(1−m)⋅0.04+m⋅baseColor

    where m∈[0,1]m \in [0,1]m∈[0,1] is metallic.

    Diffuse base term becomes approximately:

    cdiff=(1−m)⋅baseColorc_{diff} = (1-m)\cdot \text{baseColor}cdiff​=(1−m)⋅baseColor

    3.3 Roughness

    Roughness controls microfacet distribution width.

    Usually define:

    α=roughness2\alpha = roughness^2α=roughness2

    Squaring gives better perceptual response and stabilizes the NDF.

    3.4 Specular term

    The microfacet Cook-Torrance form:

    fs=D(h) F(v,h) G(l,v)4(n⋅l)(n⋅v)f_s = \frac{D(\mathbf{h})\,F(\mathbf{v},\mathbf{h})\,G(\mathbf{l},\mathbf{v})} {4(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{l})(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{v})}fs​=4(n⋅l)(n⋅v)D(h)F(v,h)G(l,v)​

    where:

    • h=l+v∥l+v∥\mathbf{h} = \frac{\mathbf{l}+\mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{l}+\mathbf{v}\|}h=∥l+v∥l+v​
    • DDD: normal distribution
    • FFF: Fresnel
    • GGG: masking-shadowing

    3.5 GGX NDF

    DGGX=α2π((n⋅h)2(α2−1)+1)2D_{GGX} = \frac{\alpha^2} {\pi \left((n\cdot h)^2(\alpha^2-1)+1\right)^2}DGGX​=π((n⋅h)2(α2−1)+1)2α2​

    This gives wide realistic tails.

    3.6 Schlick Fresnel

    F=F0+(1−F0)(1−v⋅h)5F = F_0 + (1-F_0)(1-v\cdot h)^5F=F0​+(1−F0​)(1−v⋅h)5

    For metals, F0F_0F0​ is colored.
    For dielectrics, F0≈0.04F_0\approx 0.04F0​≈0.04.

    3.7 Smith geometry term

    Common glTF-ish approximation:

    G=G1(n⋅l) G1(n⋅v)G = G_1(n\cdot l)\, G_1(n\cdot v)G=G1​(n⋅l)G1​(n⋅v)

    with Schlick-GGX form:

    G1(x)=xx(1−k)+kG_1(x) = \frac{x}{x(1-k)+k}G1​(x)=x(1−k)+kx​

    where often

    k=(α+1)28k = \frac{(\alpha + 1)^2}{8}k=8(α+1)2​

    or some related roughness-derived approximation depending on direct vs IBL path.

    3.8 Diffuse term

    For core glTF, diffuse is Lambertian-ish:

    fd=cdiffπf_d = \frac{c_{diff}}{\pi}fd​=πcdiff​​

    Not Disney diffuse in core spec.

    4. Tangent-space normal mapping derivation

    This is one of the easiest concepts to use and one of the most commonly misunderstood mathematically.

    4.1 Problem

    A normal map stores local perturbations relative to the surface parameterization, not in world space.

    Each texel gives a normal in tangent space:

    • +x+x+x along tangent TTT
    • +y+y+y along bitangent BBB
    • +z+z+z along geometric normal NNN

    So we need a matrix TBNTBNTBN to convert sampled normal to world/view space.

    4.2 Surface parameterization

    A mesh surface can be parameterized by texture coordinates (u,v)(u,v)(u,v).

    The surface position is:

    p(u,v)\mathbf{p}(u,v)p(u,v)

    Then the tangent basis directions are:

    t=∂p∂u,b=∂p∂v\mathbf{t} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial u}, \qquad \mathbf{b} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial v}t=∂u∂p​,b=∂v∂p​

    And geometric normal:

    n=t×b∥t×b∥\mathbf{n} = \frac{\mathbf{t} \times \mathbf{b}}{\|\mathbf{t}\times\mathbf{b}\|}n=∥t×b∥t×b​

    4.3 Triangle-based derivation

    For triangle vertices p0,p1,p2p_0,p_1,p_2p0​,p1​,p2​ with UVs (u0,v0),(u1,v1),(u2,v2)(u_0,v_0),(u_1,v_1),(u_2,v_2)(u0​,v0​),(u1​,v1​),(u2​,v2​), let:

    Δp1=p1−p0,Δp2=p2−p0\Delta p_1 = p_1-p_0,\quad \Delta p_2 = p_2-p_0Δp1​=p1​−p0​,Δp2​=p2​−p0​ Δuv1=(u1−u0, v1−v0),Δuv2=(u2−u0, v2−v0)\Delta uv_1 = (u_1-u_0,\ v_1-v_0),\quad \Delta uv_2 = (u_2-u_0,\ v_2-v_0)Δuv1​=(u1​−u0​, v1​−v0​),Δuv2​=(u2​−u0​, v2​−v0​)

    Assume:

    Δp1=tΔu1+bΔv1\Delta p_1 = \mathbf{t}\Delta u_1 + \mathbf{b}\Delta v_1Δp1​=tΔu1​+bΔv1​ Δp2=tΔu2+bΔv2\Delta p_2 = \mathbf{t}\Delta u_2 + \mathbf{b}\Delta v_2Δp2​=tΔu2​+bΔv2​

    In matrix form:

    [Δp1 Δp2]=[t b][Δu1Δu2Δv1Δv2][\Delta p_1\ \Delta p_2] = [\mathbf{t}\ \mathbf{b}] \begin{bmatrix} \Delta u_1 & \Delta u_2\\ \Delta v_1 & \Delta v_2 \end{bmatrix}[Δp1​ Δp2​]=[t b][Δu1​Δv1​​Δu2​Δv2​​]

    So:

    [t b]=[Δp1 Δp2][Δu1Δu2Δv1Δv2]−1[\mathbf{t}\ \mathbf{b}] = [\Delta p_1\ \Delta p_2] \begin{bmatrix} \Delta u_1 & \Delta u_2\\ \Delta v_1 & \Delta v_2 \end{bmatrix}^{-1}[t b]=[Δp1​ Δp2​][Δu1​Δv1​​Δu2​Δv2​​]−1

    For 2x2 inverse:

    r=1Δu1Δv2−Δu2Δv1r = \frac{1}{\Delta u_1 \Delta v_2 - \Delta u_2 \Delta v_1}r=Δu1​Δv2​−Δu2​Δv1​1​

    Then:

    t=r(Δp1Δv2−Δp2Δv1)\mathbf{t} = r(\Delta p_1 \Delta v_2 - \Delta p_2 \Delta v_1)t=r(Δp1​Δv2​−Δp2​Δv1​) b=r(−Δp1Δu2+Δp2Δu1)\mathbf{b} = r(-\Delta p_1 \Delta u_2 + \Delta p_2 \Delta u_1)b=r(−Δp1​Δu2​+Δp2​Δu1​)

    4.4 From normal map RGB to tangent-space vector

    A normal map texel in [0,1]3[0,1]^3[0,1]3 is decoded to [−1,1]3[-1,1]^3[−1,1]3:

    nt=2c−1\mathbf{n}_t = 2\mathbf{c} - 1nt​=2c−1

    Then transform:

    nw=normalize⁡(TBNnt)\mathbf{n}_w = \operatorname{normalize}(TBN \mathbf{n}_t)nw​=normalize(TBNnt​)

    where

    TBN=[t^ b^ n^]TBN = [\hat{\mathbf{t}}\ \hat{\mathbf{b}}\ \hat{\mathbf{n}}]TBN=[t^ b^ n^]

    4.5 Orthonormalization

    Tangents computed from UV derivatives are not guaranteed orthogonal to the normal. So use Gram-Schmidt:

    t′=normalize⁡(t−n(n⋅t))\mathbf{t}' = \operatorname{normalize}(\mathbf{t} - \mathbf{n}(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{t}))t′=normalize(t−n(n⋅t)) b′=sign⁡⋅(n×t′)\mathbf{b}' = \operatorname{sign} \cdot (\mathbf{n} \times \mathbf{t}')b′=sign⋅(n×t′)

    The sign handles mirrored UVs / handedness.

    Often tangent attribute is stored as vec4:

    • xyz = tangent
    • w = handedness sign

    Then:

    b=w(n×t)\mathbf{b} = w(\mathbf{n}\times\mathbf{t})b=w(n×t)

    5. Skinning math and inverse bind matrices

    Now animation.

    5.1 Basic idea

    A vertex in bind pose is attached to bones/joints with weights.

    At runtime, each joint has moved relative to bind pose.
    We apply weighted transforms.

    5.2 Spaces involved

    • mesh bind/object space
    • joint local space
    • world space
    • joint bind space

    The inverse bind matrix is the crucial “undo bind pose” transform.

    5.3 Bind pose relation

    Let:

    • BiB_iBi​ = joint iii’s world transform in bind pose
    • Mi(t)M_i(t)Mi​(t) = joint iii’s current world transform at time ttt

    A vertex ppp influenced by joint iii:

    1. move from mesh bind position into joint bind-local space:
    pjoint=Bi−1pp_{joint} = B_i^{-1} ppjoint​=Bi−1​p
    1. move by current joint world transform:
    pi′(t)=Mi(t)Bi−1pp_i'(t) = M_i(t) B_i^{-1} ppi′​(t)=Mi​(t)Bi−1​p

    Thus the skinning matrix is:

    Si(t)=Mi(t)Bi−1S_i(t) = M_i(t) B_i^{-1}Si​(t)=Mi​(t)Bi−1​

    5.4 Linear blend skinning

    With weights wiw_iwi​, ∑iwi=1\sum_i w_i = 1∑i​wi​=1:

    p′(t)=∑iwiSi(t)pp'(t) = \sum_i w_i S_i(t) pp′(t)=i∑​wi​Si​(t)p

    In homogeneous form:

    p~′(t)=∑iwiMi(t)Bi−1p~\tilde p'(t) = \sum_i w_i M_i(t) B_i^{-1} \tilde pp~​′(t)=i∑​wi​Mi​(t)Bi−1​p~​

    Normals use the linear part appropriately, often approximated similarly then renormalized.

    5.5 Why inverse bind matrices matter

    Without Bi−1B_i^{-1}Bi−1​, you’d apply current joint transforms directly to a vertex that is not expressed in that joint’s bind-local coordinates. The result would be nonsense or double transforms.

    Inverse bind matrix means:

    • “remove the bind pose relationship”
    • then apply current pose

    This is exactly the change of basis needed.

    5.6 Bone hierarchy

    A joint’s world transform is:

    Mi=Mparent(i)⋅LiM_i = M_{parent(i)} \cdot L_iMi​=Mparent(i)​⋅Li​

    where LiL_iLi​ is local TRS for the joint.

    So animated skeleton evaluation is recursive / topologically ordered.

    6. PMREM / split-sum approximation for IBL

    This is where real-time PBR gets clever.

    The full specular IBL integral is expensive:

    Lo(v)=∫Ω+fs(l,v)Li(l)(n⋅l)dωL_o(\mathbf{v}) = \int_{\Omega^+} f_s(\mathbf{l},\mathbf{v}) L_i(\mathbf{l})(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{l}) d\omegaLo​(v)=∫Ω+​fs​(l,v)Li​(l)(n⋅l)dω

    Since fsf_sfs​ depends on:

    • normal
    • view direction
    • roughness
    • Fresnel

    naively doing this per pixel is too expensive.

    6.1 Split-sum approximation

    We approximate the integral by splitting it into:

    1. prefiltered environment map
    2. BRDF integration LUT

    So:

    Lspec≈PrefilteredEnv(R,α)⋅BRDFLUT(n⋅v,α)L_{spec} \approx \text{PrefilteredEnv}(R,\alpha) \cdot \text{BRDFLUT}(n\cdot v,\alpha)Lspec​≈PrefilteredEnv(R,α)⋅BRDFLUT(n⋅v,α)

    More precisely:

    Lspec≈∫DGF Li≈(prefiltered env as function of R,α)⋅(2D LUT depending on N⋅V,α)L_{spec} \approx \int DGF \, L_i \approx \left(\text{prefiltered env as function of }R,\alpha\right) \cdot \left(\text{2D LUT depending on }N\cdot V,\alpha\right)Lspec​≈∫DGFLi​≈(prefiltered env as function of R,α)⋅(2D LUT depending on N⋅V,α)

    6.2 PMREM

    PMREM = Prefiltered Mipmapped Radiance Environment Map.

    For each mip level corresponding to roughness:

    • convolve env map with approximate specular lobe width
    • store result in mip level or cube UV structure

    Then runtime:

    • compute reflection direction RRR
    • map roughness to mip level
    • sample env map at RRR, mip = roughness-dependent LOD

    Roughly:

    R=reflect(−V,N)R = reflect(-V, N)R=reflect(−V,N)

    6.3 Why a LUT is still needed

    The specular integral depends not just on roughness, but also on N⋅VN\cdot VN⋅V and Fresnel geometry factors.

    So a 2D LUT stores:

    LUT(N⋅V,α)→(A,B)\text{LUT}(N\cdot V,\alpha) \rightarrow (A,B)LUT(N⋅V,α)→(A,B)

    and specular IBL often becomes:

    Lspec≈prefilteredColor⋅(F0A+B)L_{spec} \approx prefilteredColor \cdot (F_0 A + B)Lspec​≈prefilteredColor⋅(F0​A+B)

    This is a common form of the split-sum approximation.

    7. Dissect an actual GLB file byte-by-byte

    A .glb is a binary container for glTF.

    7.1 GLB top-level structure

    A GLB file is:

    text
    [12-byte header]
[JSON chunk header]
[JSON chunk data]
[BIN chunk header]
[BIN chunk data]
[optional more chunks]

    7.2 12-byte GLB header

    Layout:

    • bytes 0..3: magic
    • bytes 4..7: version
    • bytes 8..11: total length

    All little-endian uint32.

    Magic

    ASCII "glTF":

    • 0x67 0x6C 0x54 0x46

    As uint32 little-endian:

    • 0x46546C67

    Version

    Usually 2

    Length

    Total file length in bytes

    7.3 Chunk format

    Each chunk:

    • uint32 chunkLength
    • uint32 chunkType
    • chunkData[chunkLength]

    Chunk types:

    • JSON = 0x4E4F534A ("JSON")
    • BIN = 0x004E4942 ("BIN\0")

    7.4 Example tiny GLB

    Suppose bytes begin like:

    text
    67 6C 54 46   02 00 00 00   8C 02 00 00

    Interpretation:

    • 67 6C 54 46 = "glTF"
    • 02 00 00 00 = version 2
    • 8C 02 00 00 = file length 0x028C=6520x028C = 6520x028C=652 bytes

    Then next 8 bytes might be:

    text
    90 01 00 00   4A 53 4F 4E

    Interpretation:

    • JSON chunk length = 0x0190 = 400
    • chunk type = "JSON"

    Then 400 bytes of JSON data, padded to 4-byte alignment with spaces.

    Then next chunk:

    text
    D4 00 00 00   42 49 4E 00

    Interpretation:

    • BIN chunk length = 0x00D4 = 212
    • chunk type = "BIN\0"

    Then 212 bytes of binary data.

    7.5 JSON chunk contents

    The JSON chunk may look like:

    JSON
    {
  "asset": { "version": "2.0" },
  "buffers": [{ "byteLength": 212 }],
  "bufferViews": [
    { "buffer": 0, "byteOffset": 0, "byteLength": 72, "target": 34962 },
    { "buffer": 0, "byteOffset": 72, "byteLength": 36, "target": 34963 }
  ],
  "accessors": [
    { "bufferView": 0, "componentType": 5126, "count": 6, "type": "VEC3" },
    { "bufferView": 1, "componentType": 5123, "count": 18, "type": "SCALAR" }
  ],
  "meshes": [{
    "primitives": [{
      "attributes": { "POSITION": 0 },
      "indices": 1
    }]
  }],
  "nodes": [{ "mesh": 0 }],
  "scenes": [{ "nodes": [0] }],
  "scene": 0
}

    This means:

    • one binary blob of 212 bytes
    • first 72 bytes = positions
    • next 36 bytes = indices
    • etc.

    7.6 Byte-level accessor lookup example

    Suppose accessor 0:

    JSON
    {
  "bufferView": 0,
  "componentType": 5126,
  "count": 6,
  "type": "VEC3"
}

    and bufferView 0:

    JSON
    {
  "buffer": 0,
  "byteOffset": 0,
  "byteLength": 72
}

    Then element size:

    sizeof(VEC3 FLOAT)=3⋅4=12\text{sizeof(VEC3 FLOAT)} = 3 \cdot 4 = 12sizeof(VEC3 FLOAT)=3⋅4=12

    Total expected:

    6⋅12=726 \cdot 12 = 726⋅12=72

    Element iii lives at:

    addr(i)=BIN_base+0+i⋅12addr(i) = BIN\_base + 0 + i\cdot 12addr(i)=BIN_base+0+i⋅12

    So:

    • vertex 0 = bytes 0..11
    • vertex 1 = bytes 12..23
    • etc.

    7.7 Alignment and padding

    GLB chunks are 4-byte aligned. JSON chunk is padded with ASCII spaces (0x20) typically. BIN chunk may be padded with zeros.

    This is important for safe typed array views and alignment expectations.

    8. Full glTF PBR shader math

    Let’s write a practical shading pipeline as implemented conceptually for one direct light.

    We’ll assume:

    • world/view-space vectors normalized
    • glTF metallic-roughness material
    • optional normal map
    • one direct light + IBL later

    8.1 Inputs

    Given:

    • base color cb∈[0,1]3c_b \in [0,1]^3cb​∈[0,1]3
    • metallic m∈[0,1]m \in [0,1]m∈[0,1]
    • roughness r∈[0,1]r \in [0,1]r∈[0,1]
    • normal NNN
    • view VVV
    • light direction LLL
    • half vector H=V+L∥V+L∥H = \frac{V+L}{\|V+L\|}H=∥V+L∥V+L​

    Scalars:

    N⋅L,N⋅V,N⋅H,V⋅HN\cdot L,\quad N\cdot V,\quad N\cdot H,\quad V\cdot HN⋅L,N⋅V,N⋅H,V⋅H

    Clamp all to [0,1][0,1][0,1].

    8.2 Material decomposition

    Per glTF-ish workflow:

    F0=(1−m)⋅0.04+m⋅cbF_0 = (1-m)\cdot 0.04 + m \cdot c_bF0​=(1−m)⋅0.04+m⋅cb​cdiff=(1−m)cbc_{diff} = (1-m)c_bcdiff​=(1−m)cb​α=r2\alpha = r^2α=r2

    8.3 Fresnel

    F=F0+(1−F0)(1−V⋅H)5F = F_0 + (1-F_0)(1-V\cdot H)^5F=F0​+(1−F0​)(1−V⋅H)5

    This is RGB-valued.

    8.4 NDF

    D=α2π((N⋅H)2(α2−1)+1)2D = \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi \left((N\cdot H)^2(\alpha^2-1)+1\right)^2}D=π((N⋅H)2(α2−1)+1)2α2​

    8.5 Geometry

    A common Schlick-GGX direct term:

    k=(α+1)28k = \frac{(\alpha + 1)^2}{8}k=8(α+1)2​GV=N⋅V(N⋅V)(1−k)+kG_V = \frac{N\cdot V}{(N\cdot V)(1-k)+k}GV​=(N⋅V)(1−k)+kN⋅V​ GL=N⋅L(N⋅L)(1−k)+kG_L = \frac{N\cdot L}{(N\cdot L)(1-k)+k}GL​=(N⋅L)(1−k)+kN⋅L​ G=GVGLG = G_V G_LG=GV​GL​

    8.6 Specular BRDF

    fs=DGF4(N⋅L)(N⋅V)+ϵf_s = \frac{DGF}{4(N\cdot L)(N\cdot V)+\epsilon}fs​=4(N⋅L)(N⋅V)+ϵDGF​

    where ϵ\epsilonϵ prevents division blowup.

    8.7 Diffuse BRDF

    fd=cdiffπf_d = \frac{c_{diff}}{\pi}fd​=πcdiff​​

    Sometimes modulated by 1−F1-F1−F-ish energy split, depending on implementation:

    kD=(1−F)(1−m)k_D = (1-F)(1-m)kD​=(1−F)(1−m) fd=kDcbπf_d = \frac{k_D c_b}{\pi}fd​=πkD​cb​​

    This better preserves energy near grazing angles.

    8.8 Direct light contribution

    For incident radiance LiL_iLi​ from light:

    Lodirect=(fd+fs)Li(N⋅L)L_o^{direct} = (f_d + f_s) L_i (N\cdot L)Lodirect​=(fd​+fs​)Li​(N⋅L)

    If the light has color clc_lcl​ and intensity III, then:

    Li=Icl⋅attenuationL_i = I c_l \cdot attenuationLi​=Icl​⋅attenuation

    For punctual lights, attenuation depends on distance and range.

    8.9 Occlusion / emissive

    Emissive adds directly:

    Lo←Lo+cemissiveL_o \leftarrow L_o + c_{emissive}Lo​←Lo​+cemissive​

    Occlusion texture usually modulates indirect lighting, not direct:

    Lindirect←ao⋅LindirectL_{indirect} \leftarrow ao \cdot L_{indirect}Lindirect​←ao⋅Lindirect​

    9. Show how to add custom GLSL to the Three.js viewer

    Now the fun part.

    We’ll do this at three levels:

    1. Tiny patch via onBeforeCompile
    2. Add custom uniforms
    3. Patch both vertex and fragment stages
    4. Optional fully custom ShaderMaterial while keeping GLB geometry

    9.1 Minimal fragment patch

    Suppose after loading the GLB you want every mesh to get a view-dependent rim light.

    Add this after modelRoot is loaded:

    JavaScript
    function addRimLighting(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;

    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      if (!mat || !mat.isMeshStandardMaterial) continue;

      mat.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <output_fragment>',
          `
            float rim = pow(1.0 - max(dot(normalize(vNormal), normalize(vViewPosition)), 0.0), 2.0);
            outgoingLight += vec3(0.2, 0.5, 1.0) * rim * 0.35;
            #include <output_fragment>
          `
        );
      };

      mat.needsUpdate = true;
    }
  });
}

    Then call:

    JavaScript
    addRimLighting(modelRoot);

    9.2 Why this works

    Inside Three’s standard PBR fragment shader:

    • outgoingLight is the current final linear RGB before output conversion
    • vNormal and vViewPosition are available varyings in many material variants

    Rim term:

    rim=(1−max⁡(0,N⋅V))2rim = (1 - \max(0, N\cdot V))^2rim=(1−max(0,N⋅V))2

    High when view is grazing the surface.

    9.3 Better version with custom uniforms and time animation

    JavaScript
    function addAnimatedGlow(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;

    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      if (!mat || !mat.isMeshStandardMaterial) continue;

      mat.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
        shader.uniforms.uTime = { value: 0.0 };
        shader.uniforms.uGlowColor = { value: new THREE.Color(0xff44aa) };

        mat.userData.shader = shader;

        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <common>',
          `
            #include <common>
            uniform float uTime;
            uniform vec3 uGlowColor;
          `
        );

        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <output_fragment>',
          `
            float rim = pow(1.0 - max(dot(normalize(vNormal), normalize(vViewPosition)), 0.0), 3.0);
            float pulse = 0.5 + 0.5 * sin(uTime * 2.0);
            outgoingLight += uGlowColor * rim * pulse * 0.5;
            #include <output_fragment>
          `
        );
      };

      mat.needsUpdate = true;
    }
  });
}

    Then in your render loop:

    JavaScript
    const t = clock.elapsedTime;

if (modelRoot) {
  modelRoot.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;
    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      const shader = mat.userData.shader;
      if (shader) shader.uniforms.uTime.value = t;
    }
  });
}

    9.4 Vertex displacement patch

    Now patch vertex stage too.

    This adds a pulsating offset along the normal.

    JavaScript
    function addVertexWobble(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;

    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      if (!mat || !mat.isMeshStandardMaterial) continue;

      mat.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
        shader.uniforms.uTime = { value: 0.0 };
        mat.userData.shader = shader;

        shader.vertexShader = shader.vertexShader.replace(
          '#include <common>',
          `
            #include <common>
            uniform float uTime;
          `
        );

        shader.vertexShader = shader.vertexShader.replace(
          '#include <begin_vertex>',
          `
            #include <begin_vertex>
            transformed += normal * (0.03 * sin(uTime * 3.0 + position.y * 8.0));
          `
        );

        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <output_fragment>',
          `
            float rim = pow(1.0 - max(dot(normalize(vNormal), normalize(vViewPosition)), 0.0), 2.0);
            outgoingLight += vec3(0.1, 0.4, 1.0) * rim * 0.2;
            #include <output_fragment>
          `
        );
      };

      mat.needsUpdate = true;
    }
  });
}

    Note:

    • transformed is a standard intermediate variable in many Three vertex shaders
    • normal here is object-space normal before all transforms

    9.5 Caveat: normals after displacement

    If you displace vertices in the vertex shader but leave normals unchanged, lighting becomes approximate/wrong. For small deformations, acceptable. For strong displacement, you must recompute or approximate modified normals.

    This is a major real shader issue.

    9.6 Full custom ShaderMaterial with GLB geometry

    You can also abandon the standard material and keep only GLB geometry.

    Example:

    JavaScript
    const customMat = new THREE.ShaderMaterial({
  uniforms: {
    uTime: { value: 0 },
  },
  vertexShader: `
    varying vec3 vNormalW;
    varying vec3 vPosW;
    uniform float uTime;

    void main() {
      vec3 p = position + normal * (0.05 * sin(uTime + position.y * 10.0));
      vec4 worldPos = modelMatrix * vec4(p, 1.0);
      vPosW = worldPos.xyz;
      vNormalW = normalize(mat3(modelMatrix) * normal);
      gl_Position = projectionMatrix * viewMatrix * worldPos;
    }
  `,
  fragmentShader: `
    varying vec3 vNormalW;
    varying vec3 vPosW;
    uniform float uTime;

    void main() {
      vec3 N = normalize(vNormalW);
      vec3 V = normalize(cameraPosition - vPosW);
      float rim = pow(1.0 - max(dot(N, V), 0.0), 3.0);
      vec3 base = vec3(0.1, 0.2, 0.9);
      vec3 color = base + rim * vec3(1.0, 0.4, 0.9);
      gl_FragColor = vec4(color, 1.0);
    }
  `
});

    Then after loading:

    JavaScript
    modelRoot.traverse((obj) => {
  if (obj.isMesh) obj.material = customMat;
});

    But you lose:

    • glTF PBR fidelity
    • automatic texture handling
    • env lighting integration
    • skinning/morph support unless you add them explicitly

    So onBeforeCompile is often the sweet spot.

    Bonus: a more serious custom GLSL patch for glTF PBR

    Let’s add a curvature-ish / world-space stripe debug effect while preserving PBR.

    JavaScript
    function addWorldStripeEffect(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;

    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      if (!mat || !mat.isMeshStandardMaterial) continue;

      mat.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
        shader.uniforms.uStripeScale = { value: 12.0 };
        shader.uniforms.uStripeColor = { value: new THREE.Color(0x00e5ff) };
        mat.userData.shader = shader;

        shader.vertexShader = shader.vertexShader.replace(
          '#include <common>',
          `
            #include <common>
            varying vec3 vWorldPosCustom;
          `
        );

        shader.vertexShader = shader.vertexShader.replace(
          '#include <worldpos_vertex>',
          `
            #include <worldpos_vertex>
            vWorldPosCustom = worldPosition.xyz;
          `
        );

        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <common>',
          `
            #include <common>
            uniform float uStripeScale;
            uniform vec3 uStripeColor;
            varying vec3 vWorldPosCustom;
          `
        );

        shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
          '#include <output_fragment>',
          `
            float stripe = 0.5 + 0.5 * sin(vWorldPosCustom.y * uStripeScale);
            stripe = smoothstep(0.45, 0.55, stripe);
            outgoingLight = mix(outgoingLight, uStripeColor, stripe * 0.15);
            #include <output_fragment>
          `
        );
      };

      mat.needsUpdate = true;
    }
  });
}

    This preserves all the base PBR shading but overlays a procedural effect.

    Tying the topics together

    Here is the complete chain conceptually:

    glb bytes

    store:

    • JSON metadata
    • binary vertex/index/animation data

    GLTFLoader

    interprets:

    • buffers → bufferViews → accessors
    • meshes/materials/skins/animations

    Three.js

    constructs:

    • BufferGeometry
    • MeshStandardMaterial
    • SkinnedMesh
    • animation clips

    WebGLRenderer

    compiles:

    • shader variants based on material features and scene state

    PBR shader

    evaluates:

    • metallic-roughness microfacet BRDF
    • normal mapping via TBN
    • skinning via weighted joint matrices
    • IBL via PMREM split-sum approximation

    Custom GLSL hooks

    let you surgically alter the generated shader

    If you want the next truly hardcore steps

    I can continue with any of these:

    1. Take a sample GLB and manually decode its header + JSON + first accessor in code
    2. Write the actual GLSL for a simplified glTF metallic-roughness shader
    3. Show Three.js generated shader source and annotate it line by line
    4. Add custom PBR terms like clearcoat/rim/subsurface-ish hacks to the viewer
    5. Explain KHR_materials extensions mathematically
    6. Build a shader debugger view: normals, roughness, metalness, UVs, tangent basis, AO

    If you want “gory technical”, I’d suggest next doing:
    (2) actual GLSL for simplified glTF PBR and (6) shader debugger view.

    Message from Assistant A

    yes pls let's get into all 6 Given your request for technical depth, the next best topics are:

    Three.js material/shader compilation pipeline
    glTF binary layout: buffers, bufferViews, accessors
    PBR equations in glTF metallic-roughness
    Tangent-space normal mapping derivation
    Skinning math and inverse bind matrices
    PMREM / split-sum approximation for IBL
    and all 3 of Dissect an actual GLB file byte-by-byte
    Explain the full glTF PBR shader math
    Show how to add custom GLSL to the Three.js viewer PLS!

    Assistant A

    Advanced glossary — dense, math-forward

    Below, x∈Rn\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^nx∈Rn, matrices are column-major by convention unless noted, homogeneous points are p~=(x,y,z,1)T\tilde{\mathbf{p}}=(x,y,z,1)^Tp~​=(x,y,z,1)T, directions (x,y,z,0)T(x,y,z,0)^T(x,y,z,0)T.

    Coordinate spaces

    • Object/local space
      Vertex coordinates in a mesh’s own basis. A point transforms to world via p~w=Mp~o\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_w = M \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_op~​w​=Mp~​o​.

    • World space
      Shared scene frame after model transforms.

    • View/eye/camera space
      Coordinates after camera inverse transform:
      p~v=Vp~w\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_v = V \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_wp~​v​=Vp~​w​, where V=C−1V = C^{-1}V=C−1.

    • Clip space
      After projection: p~c=Pp~v\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_c = P \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_vp~​c​=Pp~​v​. Visibility before divide is tested against
      −w≤x,y,z≤w-w \le x,y,z \le w−w≤x,y,z≤w in OpenGL-style conventions.

    • NDC
      Normalized device coordinates after perspective divide:
      pndc=(xc/wc, yc/wc, zc/wc)\mathbf{p}_{ndc}=(x_c/w_c,\ y_c/w_c,\ z_c/w_c)pndc​=(xc​/wc​, yc​/wc​, zc​/wc​).

    • Screen/window space
      Viewport map from NDC to pixels:
      xs=wv2(xndc+1)+x0x_s = \frac{w_v}{2}(x_{ndc}+1)+x_0xs​=2wv​​(xndc​+1)+x0​,
      ys=hv2(yndc+1)+y0y_s = \frac{h_v}{2}(y_{ndc}+1)+y_0ys​=2hv​​(yndc​+1)+y0​.

    Linear algebra / transforms

    • Affine transform
      p′=Ap+t\mathbf{p}' = A\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{t}p′=Ap+t. In homogeneous form

      T=[At01].T=\begin{bmatrix}A & \mathbf{t}\\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}.T=[A0​t1​].

      Preserves parallelism, not necessarily lengths/angles.

    • Rigid transform / SE(3)
      A=R∈SO(3)A=R \in SO(3)A=R∈SO(3), RTR=I,det⁡R=1R^TR=I, \det R=1RTR=I,detR=1. Translation + rotation only.

    • Quaternion
      q=(w,v)q=(w,\mathbf{v})q=(w,v), unit norm ∥q∥=1\|q\|=1∥q∥=1. Rotates vector x\mathbf{x}x via
      x′=q (0,x) q−1\mathbf{x}' = q\, (0,\mathbf{x})\, q^{-1}x′=q(0,x)q−1.
      More numerically stable than Euler for interpolation.

    • SLERP
      Spherical interpolation between unit quaternions q0,q1q_0,q_1q0​,q1​:

      slerp⁡(q0,q1;t)=sin⁡((1−t)Ω)sin⁡Ωq0+sin⁡(tΩ)sin⁡Ωq1,cos⁡Ω=q0⋅q1.\operatorname{slerp}(q_0,q_1;t)= \frac{\sin((1-t)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}q_0+ \frac{\sin(t\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}q_1, \quad \cos\Omega=q_0\cdot q_1.slerp(q0​,q1​;t)=sinΩsin((1−t)Ω)​q0​+sinΩsin(tΩ)​q1​,cosΩ=q0​⋅q1​.

    • Normal matrix
      Normals transform by inverse-transpose of the linear part:

      n′∝(A−1)Tn.\mathbf{n}' \propto (A^{-1})^T \mathbf{n}.n′∝(A−1)Tn.

      If nonuniform scale exists, using AAA directly is wrong.

    Projection / camera

    • Perspective projection
      Standard pinhole model; in one convention:

      P=[f/aspect0000f0000f+nn−f2fnn−f00−10],f=cot⁡(fov2).P= \begin{bmatrix} f/aspect & 0 & 0 & 0\\ 0 & f & 0 & 0\\ 0 & 0 & \frac{f+n}{n-f} & \frac{2fn}{n-f}\\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad f=\cot(\tfrac{\text{fov}}{2}).P=​f/aspect000​0f00​00n−ff+n​−1​00n−f2fn​0​​,f=cot(2fov​).

      Encodes foreshortening via divide by www.

    • Near/far plane precision pathology
      Depth from perspective is non-linear; roughly precision concentrates near the near plane. Poor n/fn/fn/f ratio causes z-fighting. Keep near plane as large as tolerable.

    • Frustum
      Truncated pyramid in view space defining visible volume.

    Rasterization / interpolation

    • Barycentric coordinates
      For triangle vertices a,b,c\mathbf{a},\mathbf{b},\mathbf{c}a,b,c, any interior point
      p=λ0a+λ1b+λ2c\mathbf{p}=\lambda_0\mathbf{a}+\lambda_1\mathbf{b}+\lambda_2\mathbf{c}p=λ0​a+λ1​b+λ2​c,
      λi≥0, ∑λi=1\lambda_i\ge0,\ \sum\lambda_i=1λi​≥0, ∑λi​=1. Used for interpolation.

    • Perspective-correct interpolation
      Interpolate u/wu/wu/w, v/wv/wv/w, 1/w1/w1/w, then recover

      u=∑iλiui/wi∑iλi/wi.u=\frac{\sum_i \lambda_i u_i/w_i}{\sum_i \lambda_i /w_i}.u=∑i​λi​/wi​∑i​λi​ui​/wi​​.

      Linear interpolation in screen space without this is wrong under perspective.

    • Fragment vs pixel
      A fragment is a rasterization candidate carrying interpolants and coverage; it may be discarded or fail tests before becoming a final pixel.

    • Early-Z / late-Z
      Depth testing before or after fragment shading. Certain shader behaviors disable early-Z.

    Sampling / antialiasing

    • Aliasing
      High-frequency signal components folding into lower frequencies when sampled below Nyquist. In rendering: jaggies, moiré, shimmer.

    • Mipmap chain
      Pre-filtered texture pyramid L0,L1,...L_0, L_1, ...L0​,L1​,..., each level halving resolution. Approximates band-limiting for minification.

    • Trilinear filtering
      Bilinear within two adjacent mip levels, then linear between levels.

    • Anisotropic filtering
      Handles elongated projected footprints by sampling anisotropically; reduces oblique-angle blur/shimmer.

    • Texture LOD derivatives
      GPUs estimate footprint from screen-space derivatives:
      ∂u/∂x,∂u/∂y,∂v/∂x,∂v/∂y\partial u/\partial x,\partial u/\partial y,\partial v/\partial x,\partial v/\partial y∂u/∂x,∂u/∂y,∂v/∂x,∂v/∂y.

    Shading / BRDF

    • BRDF
      Bidirectional reflectance distribution function

      fr(ωi,ωo)=dLo(ωo)Li(ωi)cos⁡θi dωif_r(\omega_i,\omega_o)=\frac{dL_o(\omega_o)}{L_i(\omega_i)\cos\theta_i\, d\omega_i}fr​(ωi​,ωo​)=Li​(ωi​)cosθi​dωi​dLo​(ωo​)​

      Units: sr−1^{-1}−1. Must satisfy reciprocity and energy conservation.

    • Lambertian diffuse

      fd=ρπf_d = \frac{\rho}{\pi}fd​=πρ​

      constant BRDF. Reflected radiance proportional to max⁡(0,n⋅l)\max(0,\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{l})max(0,n⋅l).

    • Cook-Torrance microfacet BRDF
      Common PBR specular:

      fs=D(h) F(v,h) G(l,v,h)4(n⋅l)(n⋅v)f_s = \frac{D(h)\,F(v,h)\,G(l,v,h)}{4(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{l})(\mathbf{n}\cdot\mathbf{v})}fs​=4(n⋅l)(n⋅v)D(h)F(v,h)G(l,v,h)​

      with normal distribution DDD, Fresnel FFF, geometry/masking GGG.

    • GGX / Trowbridge-Reitz NDF
      Popular DDD due to long-tail highlights:

      DGGX(α,n⋅h)=α2π((n⋅h)2(α2−1)+1)2D_{GGX}(\alpha, n\cdot h)= \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi\left((n\cdot h)^2(\alpha^2-1)+1\right)^2}DGGX​(α,n⋅h)=π((n⋅h)2(α2−1)+1)2α2​

    • Schlick Fresnel approximation

      F(cos⁡θ)=F0+(1−F0)(1−cos⁡θ)5F(\cos\theta)=F_0 + (1-F_0)(1-\cos\theta)^5F(cosθ)=F0​+(1−F0​)(1−cosθ)5

      cheap approximation to full Fresnel equations.

    • Smith masking-shadowing
      Accounts for microfacet self-occlusion; often paired with GGX.

    • Energy conservation
      Reflected energy ≤\le≤ incident energy. In metallic-roughness workflows, diffuse and specular lobes are balanced to avoid >1 reflectance.

    • Linear vs sRGB space
      Lighting must occur in linear radiometric space. sRGB textures require decode clin=csrgbγc_{lin}=c_{srgb}^{\gamma}clin​=csrgbγ​-like transfer, not simple storage-as-linear. Output usually re-encoded to display transfer function.

    • Normal mapping
      Stores tangent-space normal perturbation. Needs TBN basis [T B N][T\ B\ N][T B N] to map sampled normal nt\mathbf{n}_tnt​ to world/view:

      n=normalize⁡(TBN (2t−1))\mathbf{n} = \operatorname{normalize}(TBN \, (2\mathbf{t}-1))n=normalize(TBN(2t−1))

    • Tangent space / MikkTSpace
      Consistent tangent basis generation critical for artifact-free normal mapping across tools. glTF commonly assumes MikkTSpace compatibility.

    Global illumination-ish terms

    • IBL
      Image-based lighting using environment maps. Diffuse often from irradiance convolution; specular from prefiltered env map + BRDF LUT.

    • Irradiance
      Incoming power per unit area:

      E=∫Ω+Li(ω)cos⁡θ dωE = \int_{\Omega^+} L_i(\omega)\cos\theta\, d\omegaE=∫Ω+​Li​(ω)cosθdω

    • Radiance
      Power per projected area per solid angle; the quantity largely preserved along rays in vacuum.

    • Ambient occlusion
      Heuristic/local estimate of hemispherical visibility, not full GI. In glTF occlusion texture modulates indirect lighting only by convention.

    Geometry / acceleration

    • AABB / OBB / bounding sphere
      Bounding volumes for culling/intersection. Sphere is cheap but loose; OBB tighter but costlier; AABB efficient in world-aligned broad phase.

    • BVH
      Bounding volume hierarchy; tree over geometry to reduce intersection from O(n)O(n)O(n) toward O(log⁡n)O(\log n)O(logn)-ish average.

    • Frustum culling
      Reject objects whose bounds lie outside view frustum planes.

    • Back-face culling
      Drop triangles whose orientation relative to view indicates rear side; relies on winding order.

    Animation / skinning

    • Skeletal animation
      Mesh deformation driven by bones/joints with hierarchical transforms.

    • Linear blend skinning (LBS)
      Vertex skinned by weighted sum of joint transforms:

      p~′=∑i=1kwi (MiBi−1) p~\tilde{\mathbf{p}}' = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i\, (M_i B_i^{-1})\, \tilde{\mathbf{p}}p~​′=i=1∑k​wi​(Mi​Bi−1​)p~​

      where Bi−1B_i^{-1}Bi−1​ is inverse bind matrix. Suffers candy-wrapper/collapse artifacts.

    • Dual quaternion skinning
      Better preserves rigid rotation under blending than LBS; interpolates transforms in dual quaternion space.

    • Inverse bind matrix
      Maps vertex from mesh bind pose into joint local bind space before current joint transform is applied.

    GPU / pipeline

    • VAO/VBO/EBO
      OpenGL objects for vertex layout, vertex storage, index storage. In WebGL/Three.js the abstractions differ but analogous GPU buffers exist.

    • Draw call
      CPU command to instruct GPU to render primitives with currently bound pipeline/state/resources. CPU overhead matters.

    • Instancing
      Render many copies of one mesh with per-instance transforms/attributes; amortizes draw overhead.

    • State change
      Pipeline/resource mutation. Excessive state churn hurts CPU/GPU efficiency.

    • Overdraw
      Multiple fragments shaded for same final pixel. Bad for fill-rate-limited scenes.

    • Deferred rendering
      First write G-buffer (normals, albedo, depth, etc.), then light in screen space. Efficient for many lights, awkward with transparency/MSAA.

    • Forward+ / clustered shading
      Hybrid schemes assigning lights to screen/depth bins; scalable without full deferred drawbacks.

    glTF-specific advanced terms

    • Accessor / bufferView / buffer
      glTF memory model:

      • buffer: raw binary blob
      • bufferView: slice of blob
      • accessor: typed interpretation of slice, with component type, count, shape, normalization, stride-ish usage.

    • Sparse accessor
      Stores mostly-default attribute with sparse overrides; efficient for morph deltas etc.

    • Metallic-roughness workflow
      glTF core PBR parameterization. BaseColor + metallic + roughness + normal + occlusion + emissive. Common texture packing: G=roughness, B=metallic.

    • KHR extensions
      Vendor/standard extension namespace in glTF, e.g. transmission, clearcoat, meshopt compression, Draco compression, punctual lights.

    • Draco / Meshopt compression
      Geometry compression codecs. Reduce transmission size; need decode step before GPU upload. Meshopt is often faster/runtime-friendly.

    • Quantization
      Store attributes in reduced precision, e.g. normalized 16-bit, then dequantize in shader or CPU. Trades precision for bandwidth/size.

    Gory technical Three.js + GLB viewer

    We’ll build a viewer with:

    • robust renderer config
    • physically-correct-ish color management
    • PMREM environment lighting
    • DRACO / Meshopt hooks
    • animation support
    • camera framing from bounds
    • resize / DPR handling
    • debug/perf hooks
    • some discussion of internals

    I’ll assume modern Three.js semantics.

    Architecture

    text
    GLB file
  -> GLTFLoader parses JSON/chunks/extensions
  -> decodes buffers/textures/compression
  -> creates Object3D tree, BufferGeometry, Materials, AnimationClips
  -> WebGLRenderer compiles shader programs and uploads GPU resources lazily
  -> render loop updates controls/mixer and submits draw lists

    Three.js sits above WebGL. You don’t write GLSL yet, but Three synthesizes shader programs from material features + scene lights + defines.

    Project layout

    text
    viewer/
  index.html
  main.js
  assets/
    model.glb
    env.hdr         // optional HDRI

    index.html

    HTML
    <!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1">
  <title>Gory GLB Viewer</title>
  <style>
    html, body { margin: 0; width: 100%; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #111; }
    #app { width: 100%; height: 100%; }
    #hud {
      position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; color: #ddd; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.45);
      font: 12px/1.4 monospace; padding: 10px; white-space: pre; pointer-events: none;
    }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <div id="app"></div>
  <div id="hud"></div>
  <script type="module" src="./main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

    main.js

    This version is technical but still runnable.

    JavaScript
    import * as THREE from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/build/three.module.js';

import { OrbitControls } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js';
import { GLTFLoader } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js';
import { DRACOLoader } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/loaders/DRACOLoader.js';
import { RGBELoader } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/loaders/RGBELoader.js';
import { MeshoptDecoder } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/libs/meshopt_decoder.module.js';

const app = document.getElementById('app');
const hud = document.getElementById('hud');

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Renderer
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({
  antialias: true,
  alpha: false,
  powerPreference: 'high-performance',
  preserveDrawingBuffer: false,
});

renderer.setSize(app.clientWidth, app.clientHeight);
renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2));
renderer.outputColorSpace = THREE.SRGBColorSpace;
renderer.toneMapping = THREE.ACESFilmicToneMapping;
renderer.toneMappingExposure = 1.0;
renderer.useLegacyLights = false;
renderer.physicallyCorrectLights = true;
renderer.shadowMap.enabled = true;
renderer.shadowMap.type = THREE.PCFSoftShadowMap;

app.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Scene / Camera
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const scene = new THREE.Scene();
scene.background = new THREE.Color(0x101216);

const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(
  50,
  app.clientWidth / app.clientHeight,
  0.01,
  1000
);
camera.position.set(2, 1.5, 3);

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Controls
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const controls = new OrbitControls(camera, renderer.domElement);
controls.enableDamping = true;
controls.dampingFactor = 0.08;
controls.screenSpacePanning = false;
controls.minDistance = 0.05;
controls.maxDistance = 100;

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Lights fallback (environment is preferable for PBR, but we include both)
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const hemi = new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xffffff, 0x223344, 0.35);
scene.add(hemi);

const dir = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 2.5);
dir.position.set(5, 10, 7);
dir.castShadow = true;
dir.shadow.mapSize.set(2048, 2048);
dir.shadow.camera.near = 0.1;
dir.shadow.camera.far = 50;
dir.shadow.camera.left = -10;
dir.shadow.camera.right = 10;
dir.shadow.camera.top = 10;
dir.shadow.camera.bottom = -10;
scene.add(dir);

// Ground for contact shadow context
const ground = new THREE.Mesh(
  new THREE.PlaneGeometry(50, 50),
  new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({
    color: 0x1b1d22,
    roughness: 0.95,
    metalness: 0.0
  })
);
ground.rotation.x = -Math.PI / 2;
ground.receiveShadow = true;
ground.position.y = -0.0001;
scene.add(ground);

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Environment map via HDR -> PMREM
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const pmremGen = new THREE.PMREMGenerator(renderer);
pmremGen.compileEquirectangularShader();

async function loadEnvironment(url) {
  const hdr = await new RGBELoader().loadAsync(url);
  const envRT = pmremGen.fromEquirectangular(hdr);
  hdr.dispose();
  scene.environment = envRT.texture;
  // choose whether to also use as background:
  // scene.background = envRT.texture;
}
try {
  await loadEnvironment('./assets/env.hdr');
} catch (e) {
  console.warn('No HDR environment loaded, fallback lights only.', e);
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// glTF / GLB loader with compression support
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
const gltfLoader = new GLTFLoader();

const dracoLoader = new DRACOLoader();
// If using local decoder binaries, point path there.
// dracoLoader.setDecoderPath('./draco/');
dracoLoader.setDecoderPath('https://www.gstatic.com/draco/v1/decoders/');
gltfLoader.setDRACOLoader(dracoLoader);
gltfLoader.setMeshoptDecoder(MeshoptDecoder);

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// State
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
let modelRoot = null;
let mixer = null;
const clock = new THREE.Clock();

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Utility: compute bounds and frame camera
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
function frameObject(object, camera, controls, fitOffset = 1.25) {
  const box = new THREE.Box3().setFromObject(object);
  if (box.isEmpty()) return;

  const size = new THREE.Vector3();
  const center = new THREE.Vector3();
  box.getSize(size);
  box.getCenter(center);

  const maxSize = Math.max(size.x, size.y, size.z);
  const halfFovY = THREE.MathUtils.degToRad(camera.fov * 0.5);

  // Distance required so object fits vertically.
  let distance = (maxSize * 0.5) / Math.tan(halfFovY);
  distance *= fitOffset;

  // Better framing direction than arbitrary z-only offset:
  const dir = new THREE.Vector3(1, 0.6, 1).normalize();

  camera.position.copy(center).addScaledVector(dir, distance);
  camera.near = Math.max(maxSize / 1000, 0.001);
  camera.far = Math.max(maxSize * 20, 100);
  camera.updateProjectionMatrix();

  controls.target.copy(center);
  controls.update();

  // Shadow and ground alignment
  ground.position.y = box.min.y - 0.001;
  dir.target.position.copy(center);
  dir.target.updateMatrixWorld();
  scene.add(dir.target);
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Utility: inspect materials and force sane flags
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
function sanitizeScene(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (!obj.isMesh) return;

    obj.castShadow = true;
    obj.receiveShadow = true;

    const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
    for (const mat of mats) {
      if (!mat) continue;

      // glTF loader usually sets these correctly; this is just explicit.
      mat.needsUpdate = true;

      // If you know some materials are double-sided only by export accident,
      // set FrontSide for perf. Here we leave them as authored.
      // mat.side = THREE.FrontSide;

      // Example of forensic inspection:
      // console.log(mat.name, mat.type, {
      //   colorSpaceMap: mat.map?.colorSpace,
      //   normalMap: !!mat.normalMap,
      //   roughnessMap: !!mat.roughnessMap,
      //   metalnessMap: !!mat.metalnessMap,
      //   envMapIntensity: mat.envMapIntensity
      // });
    }

    // Ensure geometry has bounds for culling and helpers
    obj.geometry.computeBoundingBox();
    obj.geometry.computeBoundingSphere();
  });
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Load GLB
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
async function loadModel(url) {
  const gltf = await gltfLoader.loadAsync(url);

  // Clean previous model
  if (modelRoot) {
    scene.remove(modelRoot);
    disposeHierarchy(modelRoot);
    modelRoot = null;
  }
  mixer = null;

  modelRoot = gltf.scene || gltf.scenes[0];
  sanitizeScene(modelRoot);
  scene.add(modelRoot);

  if (gltf.animations && gltf.animations.length > 0) {
    mixer = new THREE.AnimationMixer(modelRoot);
    // play all clips for demo purposes
    for (const clip of gltf.animations) {
      mixer.clipAction(clip).play();
    }
  }

  frameObject(modelRoot, camera, controls);

  // Dump some useful internals
  debugGLTF(gltf);
  return gltf;
}

function debugGLTF(gltf) {
  const stats = {
    nodes: 0,
    meshes: 0,
    materials: 0,
    textures: 0,
    animations: gltf.animations?.length || 0,
    triangles: 0,
    skinnedMeshes: 0,
  };

  const materialSet = new Set();
  const textureSet = new Set();

  gltf.scene.traverse((obj) => {
    stats.nodes++;

    if (obj.isMesh) {
      stats.meshes++;
      if (obj.isSkinnedMesh) stats.skinnedMeshes++;

      const geom = obj.geometry;
      if (geom.index) {
        stats.triangles += geom.index.count / 3;
      } else if (geom.attributes.position) {
        stats.triangles += geom.attributes.position.count / 3;
      }

      const mats = Array.isArray(obj.material) ? obj.material : [obj.material];
      for (const m of mats) {
        if (!m) continue;
        materialSet.add(m);
        for (const key of ['map', 'normalMap', 'roughnessMap', 'metalnessMap', 'aoMap', 'emissiveMap']) {
          if (m[key]) textureSet.add(m[key]);
        }
      }
    }
  });

  stats.materials = materialSet.size;
  stats.textures = textureSet.size;

  console.table(stats);
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Disposal to avoid leaking GPU resources when swapping models
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
function disposeMaterial(mat) {
  if (!mat) return;
  for (const key in mat) {
    const v = mat[key];
    if (v && v.isTexture) v.dispose();
  }
  mat.dispose();
}

function disposeHierarchy(root) {
  root.traverse((obj) => {
    if (obj.geometry) obj.geometry.dispose();
    if (obj.material) {
      if (Array.isArray(obj.material)) obj.material.forEach(disposeMaterial);
      else disposeMaterial(obj.material);
    }
  });
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Optional diagnostics
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
function updateHUD() {
  const info = renderer.info;
  hud.textContent =
`drawCalls: ${info.render.calls}
triangles: ${info.render.triangles}
points:    ${info.render.points}
lines:     ${info.render.lines}
geometries:${info.memory.geometries}
textures:  ${info.memory.textures}
programs:  ${info.programs ? info.programs.length : 'n/a'}

camPos:    ${camera.position.toArray().map(v => v.toFixed(3)).join(', ')}
target:    ${controls.target.toArray().map(v => v.toFixed(3)).join(', ')}`;
}

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Resize
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
function onResize() {
  const w = app.clientWidth;
  const h = app.clientHeight;
  camera.aspect = w / h;
  camera.updateProjectionMatrix();
  renderer.setSize(w, h, false);
  renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2));
}
window.addEventListener('resize', onResize);

// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
// Load and animate
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
await loadModel('./assets/model.glb');

renderer.setAnimationLoop(() => {
  const dt = clock.getDelta();
  if (mixer) mixer.update(dt);
  controls.update();
  renderer.render(scene, camera);
  updateHUD();
});

    What this is doing, technically

    1) Renderer configuration

    outputColorSpace = SRGBColorSpace

    Three.js will convert final linear framebuffer values to sRGB-ish output for display. This matters because PBR/lighting calculations are meaningful only in linear space.

    toneMapping = ACESFilmicToneMapping

    HDR lighting values can exceed displayable [0,1][0,1][0,1]. Tone mapping compresses dynamic range:

    Ldisplay=T(Lhdr)L_{display} = T(L_{hdr})Ldisplay​=T(Lhdr​)

    with a filmic curve instead of hard clipping.

    physicallyCorrectLights = true

    Uses more physically plausible falloff/intensity semantics. Not physically exact in all respects, but less ad hoc than legacy behavior.

    shadowMap

    Enables shadow map passes. Each shadow-casting light renders a depth map from light space. During shading, fragment position is transformed into light clip space and compared against stored depth.

    2) Why HDR + PMREM matters for glTF PBR

    glTF’s core material model is metallic-roughness PBR. Direct lights alone often make assets look dead or wrong. Specular reflections need a full incident radiance field.

    Three.js uses PMREM:

    • start from an environment map L(ω)L(\omega)L(ω)
    • prefilter it for roughness levels
    • during shading, sample appropriate mip based on roughness

    This approximates:

    Lo(ωo)=∫Ω+fr(ωi,ωo) Li(ωi) (n⋅ωi) dωiL_o(\omega_o) = \int_{\Omega^+} f_r(\omega_i,\omega_o)\, L_i(\omega_i)\, (\mathbf{n}\cdot\omega_i)\, d\omega_iLo​(ωo​)=∫Ω+​fr​(ωi​,ωo​)Li​(ωi​)(n⋅ωi​)dωi​

    without doing the integral per pixel at runtime.

    PMREM effectively bakes roughness-convolved specular lobes into mip levels.

    3) GLTFLoader internals, roughly

    For a .glb:

    • reads 12-byte header
    • parses JSON chunk
    • parses BIN chunk
    • resolves accessors/bufferViews into typed arrays
    • loads textures/images
    • handles extensions:
      • Draco: compressed geometry decode
      • Meshopt: compressed streams decode
      • KHR_materials_* : material extensions
      • skinning, morph targets, punctual lights, etc.

    It then constructs:

    • BufferGeometry for meshes
    • MeshStandardMaterial or related materials
    • Object3D graph for nodes
    • AnimationClip for animations
    • Skeleton and SkinnedMesh if needed

    4) Why camera framing uses bounds

    We compute an AABB:

    B=[bmin,bmax]B = [\mathbf{b}_{min}, \mathbf{b}_{max}]B=[bmin​,bmax​]

    then its center and max extent. For perspective camera with vertical FOV θ\thetaθ, the distance to fit half-height h/2h/2h/2 is

    d=h/2tan⁡(θ/2).d = \frac{h/2}{\tan(\theta/2)}.d=tan(θ/2)h/2​.

    We approximate using max dimension for a conservative fit.

    A more exact fit would project all 8 AABB corners into camera basis and solve separately for horizontal/vertical FOV constraints.

    5) GPU upload and lazy compilation

    Three.js often creates CPU-side objects immediately, but actual GPU resource creation is lazy:

    • textures upload on first use
    • buffers upload on first render
    • shader programs compile the first time a material/lighting/defines combination is encountered

    This is why first frame can hitch. Techniques:

    • call renderer.compile(scene, camera) after loading
    • warm up materials / env maps
    • keep material variants under control

    6) Material/shader permutation explosion

    A PBR material shader may vary by:

    • normal map yes/no
    • skinning yes/no
    • morph targets yes/no
    • transmission/clearcoat/sheen/etc.
    • env map yes/no
    • number/type of lights
    • shadows yes/no
    • alpha mode
    • fog/clipping/instancing

    Each combination can generate a distinct shader program. Too many permutations = compile stalls + memory overhead.

    7) Color management pitfalls

    This is one of the biggest real-world failure modes.

    Correct-ish rule

    • albedo/baseColor/emissive authored in sRGB
    • normal/roughness/metallic/occlusion authored in linear/data space
    • do all shading in linear
    • encode final output to display color space

    If you treat an sRGB albedo as linear, it appears too dark in lighting. If you gamma-correct normal maps, they break catastrophically.

    glTF + Three.js usually handles the standard cases correctly.

    8) Shadow acne / peter-panning

    Depth shadow maps compare current fragment light-space depth against stored depth. Finite precision and self-shadowing produce acne. Bias terms push comparisons:

    • too little bias → acne
    • too much bias → peter-panning / detached shadows

    You tune:

    JavaScript
    dir.shadow.bias = -0.0001;
dir.shadow.normalBias = 0.02;

    case-by-case.

    9) Animation mixer math

    Three.js evaluates animation channels over time:

    • translation: linear interpolation
    • rotation: quaternion slerp
    • scale: linear interpolation

    For skeletal animation, final skinning matrix per joint is roughly:

    Miskin=Mjoint,iworld⋅Bi−1M^{skin}_i = M^{world}_{joint,i} \cdot B_i^{-1}Miskin​=Mjoint,iworld​⋅Bi−1​

    and each vertex:

    p~′=∑i=14wiMiskinp~\tilde{\mathbf{p}}' = \sum_{i=1}^4 w_i M^{skin}_i \tilde{\mathbf{p}}p~​′=i=1∑4​wi​Miskin​p~​

    assuming 4 influences.

    10) Why Box3().setFromObject() can be expensive

    It traverses scene graph and aggregates transformed geometry bounds. Fine on load; avoid every frame on large scenes.

    If animating skinned meshes, static geometry bounds may become invalid. Per-frame exact bounds are expensive. Many engines use conservative bounds.

    Make it nastier: add interaction + picking + clipping + renderer compile

    Here are a few surgical upgrades.

    A) Precompile after load

    After scene.add(modelRoot) and environment setup:

    JavaScript
    renderer.compile(scene, camera);

    This may shift shader compile cost earlier rather than first interaction frame.

    B) Raycasting / picking

    Add:

    JavaScript
    const raycaster = new THREE.Raycaster();
const pointer = new THREE.Vector2();

window.addEventListener('pointerdown', (e) => {
  const rect = renderer.domElement.getBoundingClientRect();
  pointer.x = ((e.clientX - rect.left) / rect.width) * 2 - 1;
  pointer.y = -((e.clientY - rect.top) / rect.height) * 2 + 1;

  raycaster.setFromCamera(pointer, camera);
  const hits = modelRoot ? raycaster.intersectObject(modelRoot, true) : [];
  if (hits.length) {
    const hit = hits[0];
    console.log('Picked:', hit.object.name, hit.point, hit.faceIndex);
  }
});

    Math:

    • mouse → NDC
    • camera ray built by unprojecting through inverse PVPVPV
    • test ray against triangles/BVs

    C) Plane clipping

    Global clipping plane:

    JavaScript
    renderer.localClippingEnabled = true;
const clipPlane = new THREE.Plane(new THREE.Vector3(0, -1, 0), 0.0);
// plane equation: n·x + d = 0

    For each material:

    JavaScript
    mat.clippingPlanes = [clipPlane];

    Fragments with n⋅x+d>0\mathbf{n}\cdot \mathbf{x} + d > 0n⋅x+d>0 or opposite depending on convention are discarded. Useful for CAD/medical section views.

    D) Wireframe debug of topology stress

    JavaScript
    modelRoot.traverse((o) => {
  if (o.isMesh) {
    o.material.wireframe = true;
  }
});

    Not physically useful but exposes tessellation density and bad triangulation.

    If you want custom shader surgery without abandoning glTF

    A highly technical but practical move is material.onBeforeCompile.

    Example: inject a normal-based tint into all MeshStandardMaterials from the GLB.

    JavaScript
    modelRoot.traverse((o) => {
  if (!o.isMesh) return;
  const mats = Array.isArray(o.material) ? o.material : [o.material];

  for (const mat of mats) {
    if (!mat || !mat.isMeshStandardMaterial) continue;

    mat.onBeforeCompile = (shader) => {
      shader.fragmentShader = shader.fragmentShader.replace(
        '#include <dithering_fragment>',
        `
          vec3 debugTint = normalize(vNormal) * 0.5 + 0.5;
          gl_FragColor.rgb = mix(gl_FragColor.rgb, debugTint, 0.15);
          #include <dithering_fragment>
        `
      );
    };

    mat.needsUpdate = true;
  }
});

    This is invasive and brittle but extremely useful for:

    • custom debug overlays
    • per-material hacks
    • procedural effects layered onto imported assets

    Under the hood, Three builds a shader source from chunks, and onBeforeCompile lets you mutate the generated GLSL before compile.

    glTF-specific things worth knowing in practice

    Alpha modes

    glTF materials can be:

    • OPAQUE
    • MASK
    • BLEND

    Technical implications

    • BLEND often disables depth write or complicates order-dependent transparency.
    • sorting transparent objects is approximate.
    • glass/transmission needs specialized handling; not the same as plain alpha blend.

    Tangents

    Normal maps on glTF assets look best if tangents are supplied or reconstructable. If missing, Three may compute or infer behavior, but authored tangents + consistent tangent basis are safer.

    Texture packing

    glTF often packs:

    • occlusion in R
    • roughness in G
    • metallic in B

    This minimizes fetches/bandwidth.

    Compression trade-offs

    • Draco: often smaller files, but heavier decode
    • Meshopt: usually faster decode and GPU-friendly order optimization
    • both are transmission-side wins but can shift cost to client CPU

    Performance checklist for a GLB viewer

    CPU-side bottlenecks

    • too many scene nodes
    • too many draw calls
    • per-frame traversal/debug work
    • frequent bounds recomputation
    • too many shader permutations

    GPU-side bottlenecks

    • high triangle count
    • heavy overdraw/transparency
    • many shadow-casting lights
    • large shadow maps
    • expensive post-processing
    • 4K textures everywhere
    • mobile DPR too high

    Fast wins

    • clamp DPR to 1.5–2
    • reduce shadow map size
    • use one directional light
    • use HDR env instead of many direct lights
    • merge meshes / use instancing where possible
    • compress textures (KTX2/BasisU is huge, though not shown above)
    • precompile/warm-up

    Useful next upgrades to this viewer

    If you want to push it toward “serious viewer” territory:

    1. KTX2 compressed textures
    2. drag-and-drop GLB loading
    3. animation UI
    4. material inspector
    5. skeleton/bounds helpers
    6. screen-space ambient occlusion
    7. postprocessing chain
    8. ground-contact shadow blob
    9. section clipping UI
    10. GPU timing queries

    What to study next, specifically

    Given your request for technical depth, the next best topics are:

    1. Three.js material/shader compilation pipeline
    2. glTF binary layout: buffers, bufferViews, accessors
    3. PBR equations in glTF metallic-roughness
    4. Tangent-space normal mapping derivation
    5. Skinning math and inverse bind matrices
    6. PMREM / split-sum approximation for IBL

    If you want, next I can do one of these:

    1. Dissect an actual GLB file byte-by-byte
    2. Explain the full glTF PBR shader math
    3. Show how to add custom GLSL to the Three.js viewer
    4. Upgrade this viewer with drag-and-drop + material inspector + animation controls

    Message from Assistant A

    yes glossary but only for most advanced terms, and info dense and meths heavy explanations and yes 3.js glb viewer let's go, make it gory technical!

    Assistant A

    Great — here are both:

    1. a one-page cheat sheet of the GL terms
    2. a hands-on beginner path for:
      • Web: Three.js + GLB
      • Native: OpenGL + GLFW + GLSL

    1. One-page visual cheat sheet

    The big map

    text
    FILES / ASSETS
--------------
glTF   = 3D asset format
GLB    = binary single-file glTF
OBJ    = older geometry format
FBX    = Autodesk 3D format
USD    = scene interchange format

GRAPHICS APIS
-------------
OpenGL     = desktop graphics API
OpenGL ES  = mobile/embedded OpenGL
WebGL      = browser graphics API
Vulkan     = modern low-level graphics API
DirectX    = Microsoft graphics API
Metal      = Apple graphics API

SHADER LANGUAGES
----------------
GLSL   = shader language for OpenGL/WebGL
HLSL   = shader language for DirectX
WGSL   = shader language for WebGPU
MSL    = shader language for Metal
SPIR-V = shader/intermediate binary format often used with Vulkan

SUPPORT LIBRARIES
-----------------
GLFW   = window/input/OpenGL context
GLAD   = OpenGL function loader
GLEW   = older/popular OpenGL loader
SDL    = multimedia/window/input library
GLM    = math library for vectors/matrices

RENDERING CONCEPTS
------------------
Mesh        = triangles/geometry
Texture     = image data for surfaces
Material    = shading parameters
Shader      = GPU program
Buffer      = GPU memory for vertices/indices/etc.
Uniform     = shader input parameter
Camera      = view into the scene
Transform   = position/rotation/scale
PBR         = physically based rendering

    The “what does what?” table

    TermWhat it isExample use
    glTF3D asset formatStore a model with textures and animation
    GLBBinary glTF fileShip one file instead of many
    GLSLShader languageWrite lighting or texture code for GPU
    OpenGLGraphics APIRender 3D in a native app
    WebGLBrowser graphics APIRender 3D in a webpage
    GLFWWindow/context/input libraryOpen a window for OpenGL
    GLADOpenGL loaderAccess OpenGL functions
    GLMMath libraryMake matrices and vectors
    ShaderGPU programControl object appearance
    TextureImage on surfaceColor/normal/roughness maps
    PBRMaterial modelMore realistic materials

    The pipeline in one line

    text
    Blender/Maya -> export GLB/glTF -> app/engine loads it -> OpenGL/WebGL renders it -> GLSL shader colors it -> screen

    The “don’t confuse these” section

    Not the same:

    • glTF ≠ GLSL
    • GLB ≠ OpenGL
    • GLFW ≠ GLSL
    • WebGL ≠ glTF

    Correct pairings:

    • GLB/glTF = file format
    • OpenGL/WebGL = rendering API
    • GLSL = shader language
    • GLFW/GLAD = helper libraries

    The shortest possible memory trick

    text
    glTF/GLB = the 3D file
OpenGL/WebGL = the thing that draws it
GLSL = the code that decides how it looks

    2. Hands-on beginner tutorial path

    I’ll give you two paths:

    • A. Web path: easiest to get visible results
    • B. Native path: best for learning low-level graphics

    A. Web path: Three.js + GLB

    This is the fastest way to understand how these pieces work together.

    Goal

    You will:

    • create a webpage
    • load a .glb model
    • display it with a camera and light
    • understand where GLB, WebGL, and shaders fit

    What you need

    • a code editor
    • a browser
    • a simple local web server
    • one .glb model

    You can use a sample GLB from:

    • Khronos glTF sample models
    • Sketchfab downloads
    • your own Blender export

    Conceptual stack

    text
    Your code
  ↓
Three.js
  ↓
WebGL
  ↓
GPU

    And the model:

    text
    model.glb -> loaded by Three.js -> converted to GPU data -> rendered through WebGL

    Step 1: Minimal project structure

    text
    project/
  index.html
  main.js
  model.glb

    Step 2: HTML file

    HTML
    <!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8" />
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0" />
  <title>GLB Viewer</title>
  <style>
    body { margin: 0; overflow: hidden; }
    canvas { display: block; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <script type="module" src="./main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

    Step 3: JavaScript file

    Use Three.js from a CDN:

    JavaScript
    import * as THREE from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/build/three.module.js';
import { GLTFLoader } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/loaders/GLTFLoader.js';
import { OrbitControls } from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/[email protected]/examples/jsm/controls/OrbitControls.js';

// Scene
const scene = new THREE.Scene();
scene.background = new THREE.Color(0x202025);

// Camera
const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(
  60,
  window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight,
  0.1,
  100
);
camera.position.set(2, 2, 4);

// Renderer
const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true });
renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

// Light
const hemiLight = new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xffffff, 0x444444, 1.5);
scene.add(hemiLight);

const dirLight = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 2);
dirLight.position.set(3, 5, 2);
scene.add(dirLight);

// Controls
const controls = new OrbitControls(camera, renderer.domElement);
controls.enableDamping = true;

// Optional helper
const grid = new THREE.GridHelper(10, 10);
scene.add(grid);

// Load GLB
const loader = new GLTFLoader();
loader.load(
  './model.glb',
  (gltf) => {
    const model = gltf.scene;
    scene.add(model);
  },
  (progress) => {
    console.log(`Loaded: ${((progress.loaded / progress.total) * 100).toFixed(1)}%`);
  },
  (error) => {
    console.error('Error loading GLB:', error);
  }
);

// Resize handling
window.addEventListener('resize', () => {
  camera.aspect = window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight;
  camera.updateProjectionMatrix();
  renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight);
});

// Render loop
function animate() {
  requestAnimationFrame(animate);
  controls.update();
  renderer.render(scene, camera);
}
animate();

    Step 4: Run it

    Because browsers often block local file loading, use a local server.

    Examples:

    Python

    Bash
    python -m http.server

    Then open:

    text
    http://localhost:8000

    What you just learned

    What is GLB doing?

    It stores:

    • geometry
    • materials
    • textures
    • maybe animation

    What is Three.js doing?

    It:

    • parses the GLB
    • creates scene objects
    • uploads data to GPU
    • issues draw calls

    What is WebGL doing?

    It:

    • actually talks to the GPU in the browser
    • runs shaders
    • draws pixels

    Where is GLSL here?

    Three.js uses shaders under the hood.
    You haven’t written GLSL yet, but it’s already involved.

    Step 5: Add animation if present

    If your GLB has animation:

    JavaScript
    let mixer = null;
const clock = new THREE.Clock();

loader.load('./model.glb', (gltf) => {
  const model = gltf.scene;
  scene.add(model);

  if (gltf.animations.length > 0) {
    mixer = new THREE.AnimationMixer(model);
